stem: For startup valuation, the base rate for a tech startup depends on which reference class is used: Reference Class A (all venture-backed startups, n=1000): base rate = 17%. Reference Class B (SaaS startups at Series B, n=50): base rate = 46%. Reference Class C (startups in this specific niche, n=30): base rate = 65%. Each reference class has a different level of specificity and sample size. What is the appropriate base rate to use, and how should the choice of reference class affect the uncertainty in the prediction?

ground_truth.answer: The reference class problem: different classes give rates of 17%, 46%, 65%. No single class is objectively 'correct'. Combined interval spanning all classes: [0.148, 0.795]. Weighted point estimate (specificity-weighted): 0.447.

solution_steps: Class A (all venture-backed startups): rate=0.17, n=1000, CI=[0.148, 0.195]. Large sample but least specific.
Class B (SaaS startups at Series B): rate=0.46, n=50, CI=[0.33, 0.596]. Moderate specificity and sample size.
Class C (startups in this specific niche): rate=0.65, n=30, CI=[0.471, 0.795]. Most specific but smallest sample (widest CI).
The reference class problem has no definitive solution — the 'correct' base rate depends on which class membership is considered most relevant.
Full uncertainty range spanning all reference classes: [0.148, 0.795].
common_failure_modes: Choosing one reference class without acknowledging the others
Using only the most specific class (Class C) despite its small sample size
Using only the broadest class (Class A) despite its low relevance
Not recognizing that the choice of reference class is itself a source of fundamental uncertainty
Not considering that the reference classes may overlap, complicating the combination
